content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Help me pick a scary movie to watch with a bunch of 14-15yr olds

**Score:** 53
**Body:** So my kids and I do a scary movie night (watch movies, order pizza, eat candy) on Halloween because they were never really into trick or treating. This year my kid's friends have asked to join. I'm not sure which movie to pick. 

I was thinking Signs because it's my favorite but we watched it last year. Then I was thinking Blair Witch because this generation loves found footage but, I feel like it was only really scary when I was their age because everyone thought it was real. I was also thinking Stigmata just because it's good and I haven't seen it in forever. I dunnooooo.. Help!



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Poltergeist
  - Poltergeist 2, Reverend Kane still scares me and I'm 45.
2. Tucker & Dale vs.Evil
3. Cabin in the woods is a fun one! Maybe watch before to see if it's appropriate but it's solid lol
4. Disturbia is pretty good.
5. With younger people I'd suggest something fun like Cabin in The Woods, Drag Me to Hell, You're Next. When I was that age Spike TV would do a marathon of the Friday the 13th movies, you could do that, but I would start with Part3 cause that's where Jason starts wearing the hockey mask.
6. Hmm.. Super 8? Kinda scary in places.

The Village is another creepy Shyamalan movie.
7. Alien is always a great pick.

Or:

The Descent

Scream

The Ring

The Conjuring
8. Arachnophobia? I remember watching that as a kid and then we all went to sleep in the caravan in the backyard. Everyone tried to act cool but it was a windy stormy night and there were plenty of cobwebs in the old caravan. The parents woke up in the morning to a bunch of kids sleeping on the kitchen floor.
9. Signs, The Blair Witch Project and Stigmata are all solid choices. Of the three I would go BWP. Here are some others that might work:

The Others (2001)

Coraline (2009)

A Quiet Place (2018)

Poltergeist (1982)

Goosebumps (2015)

## 2. BEST FOREIGN HORROR MOVIES

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I'm creating a watchlist of must-watch horror movies so I can binge-watch them in October! I need some recommendations so I can make the list less repetitive and more unique! I have always wanted to watch the best Japanese movies, but I'm not really familiar with them. Brazilian movies are appreciated too! (I'm Brazilian lol) 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Train to Busan (Korean)
2. Martyrs - 2008 (French)

When Evil Lurks - 2023 (Argentinian)

Ringu - 1998 (Japan)

The Wailing - 2016 (South Korea)
3. Suspiria (1977) - Italia

Let The Right One In (2008) - Swede
4. Tetsuo: The Iron Man is a BATSHIT INSANE Japanese body horror. If you like Eraserhead and Videodrome, this movie is like a mix of both, but somehow even weirder. There's a part where a guy's dong turns into a giant drill and it's awesome.
5. The Orphanage (2007)
6. Pulse (2001)
7. Audition
8. A Girl Walks Home Alone at Night (2014). It’s an Iranian vampire western shot in black and white.
9. Cure
10. If you like Brazilian movies, the Coffin Joe series is super popular there

The first and second one are pretty good, third is a lot more iffy. There's also a bunch of quasi related movies where the character of Coffin Joe appears, but they're not considered part of the main series.

## 3. I like movies following a grounded investigation.

**Score:** 19
**Body:** And not necessarily by the police—I prefer movies following writers, especially reporters. My favorite like this are Zodiac, Spotlight, and All The President’s Men. What else would I like?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Outsider

Chinatown

True detectives s1 & s2

Goliath
2. Michael Clayton
3. Memories of Murder
4. Citizen Kane

Nightcrawler
5. The insider (1999).  

Dark waters (2019).  

Official secrets (2019 I think). 

The report (2019 also I think apparently a journo movie year)


The post (no clue when that came out )
6. The Long Goodbye (1973)

Rear Window (1984)

In The Heat of the Night (1967)
7. Erin Brockovich
8. Vengeance (2022)
9. The Pelican Brief.
  - Do NOT mix this up with The Pelican’s Briefs

## 4. Movie suggestions with no conflicts, just pure fun and enjoyment

**Score:** 15
**Body:** 


Hey everyone, I'm a bit tired of watching movies with high stakes, intense drama, or big conflicts. I'm looking for a cinematic escape—a movie that's just a pure joy to watch from beginning to end.

I'm searching for films that are essentially a cinematic vacation. Think of a movie that’s a complete low-stakes, feel-good experience. No major villains, no world-ending threats, no intense emotional drama, and no crushing third-act conflict. I just want to sit back and smile.

A good example might be something like My Neighbor Totoro or certain animated films, where the plot is simple and the focus is on wholesome moments and feeling good. What are your go-to movies for a light, fun, and conflict-free watch?



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Barb and Star Go to Vista Del Mar
2. Amelie
3. Endless Summer. A doc about Surfing. I have no interest in surfing personally. But this "movie" always makes me smile.
4. Cool Runnings
5. Napoleon Dynamite (2004)

Ferris Bueller’s Day Off (1986)
6. Clueless. 
While you were sleeping. 
Empire records. 

Maybe a TV show. Community is a great go to.
7. A League of Their Own, Clue, Singing In the Rain.
8. Pooh's Heffalump Movie
9. Flow is an animated movie that follows a cat as it navigates the changes happening around it.  No words.  Just some images and music and a nice peaceful vibe.

## 5. Guys what are some detective movies where the viewer can't solve the puzzle till the very end?

**Score:** 14
**Body:** My mother is a fan of detective movies and she is looking for a movie in which you wouldn't be able to solve the puzzle, bonus points if it doesn't has any sex or nudity 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Clue
2. Clue
3. The Maltese Falcon
4. Usual Suspects.
  - Seconded! Amazing reveal in the last scene!
5. The Game, with Michael Douglas. Not a “detective” movie but one of those you keep guessing until the end types.
6. Knives Out, Glas Onion.  
Modern movies done in the classic Agatha Christie style.
7. Anything Agatha Christie.
8. Deathtrap
9. Shutter Island

2. RIP Michelle Trachtenberg…

3. robot fight 😂
4. Excellent dumb comedy. This is Bratislava!
5. Absolutely a yay
6. It’s probably the best of the sex comedy films that popped up after American Pie. But when it’s written by the creators of Veep and Barry, it’s probably going to be fun
7. Scotty doesn't know that Fiona and me
Do it in my van every Sunday
She tells him she's in church but she doesn't go
Still she's on her knees and Scotty doesn't know
Oh, Scotty doesn't know
So don't tell Scotty
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
(So don't tell Scotty)
Fiona says she's out shopping
But she's under me
And I'm not stopping
'Cause Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
So don't tell Scotty
Scotty doesn't know
Don't tell Scotty
I can't believe he's so trusting
While I'm right behind you thrusting
Fiona's got him on the phone
And she's trying not to moan
It's a three-way call and he knows nothing, nothing
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
So don't tell Scotty
'Cause Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
So don't tell Scotty
We'll put on a show
Everyone will go
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
The parkin' lot, why not?
It's so cool when you're on top
His front lawn in the snow
Life is so hard 'cause Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
I did her on his birthday
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
So don't tell Scotty
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty will know
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty's gotta know
I'm gonna tell Scotty
Gonna tell him myself
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty has to
Scotty has to
Scotty has to go
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
(So don't tell Scotty)
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
Scotty doesn't know
(So don't tell Scotty)
Scotty doesn't know
That is so bad! So bad

## 2. Most Unexpected Cameo in a Movie

**Score:** 928
**Body:** This multiple Oscar-nominated actor in Dungeons & Dragons: Honor Among Thieves. In the following context, to have someone of his calibre play this, otherwise, insignificant role really adds to the humor. Kudos to him.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Channing Tatem in This is the End.
  - Also Channing Tatum in Bullet Train
    - Is this the sex stuff??
2. Michael Jackson in Men in Black II
  - "I COULD BE AGENT M."
3. Brad Pitt in *Deadpool 2*
  - Also Matt Damon and Alan Tudyk as the rednecks in Deadpool 2 discussing the finer points of toilet paper.
4. A recent one, George Clooney in The Flash. Heard actual gasps in the cinema, also some WTF's from the younger audience
5. Matt Damon in Ragnarok
  - Matt Damon in EuroTrip.

## 3. What is the best last line for a character....ill start

**Score:** 762
**Body:** "In case I don't see you good afternoon, good evening and good night"

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “And now we are free. I will see you again... but not yet... Not yet!” - Juba, Gladiator
2. "Louis, I think this is the beginning of a beautiful friendship."
3. “Where we’re going, we don’t need roads”
4. "Clever girl"


Robert Muldoon, Jurassic Park
5. “I hope.” Red, The Shawshank Redemption
6. HELLO BOY'S! I'M BACKKKKKKK!!
7. You’ve met me at a very strange time in my life
8. Forget it Jake, It’s Chinatown
9. "I am Iron Man"
10. Why don't we just wait here for a little while? See what happens?

McReady, 1981's the Thing

## 4. Movies with a strong idea but poor execution?

**Score:** 628
**Body:** Longlegs (2024) immediately comes to mind. I genuinely think this movie had the makings of a fantastic crime flick. Only to be let down by a disjointed story and poor character development. The first two acts are really interesting, the third was creepy but felt cheap. 

What other movies have strong ideas but poor execution? 

https://filmwaffle.com/post/is-longlegs-worth-watching-first-impressions

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The entire Purge franchise.
  - Dude, right? The first one is a fine home invasion flick with a sinister world looming in the background, then they take it to that sinister world in the 2nd one but clearly didn't have the budget or writing to make it what it totally could have been, I dropped the whole franchise after that I assume they only got worse.
2. The Creator. Incredible visuals and strong cast, but the writing wasn’t quite there.
  - The Creator is such a stunning film, but I can't say i even remember much of the story
3. In time
4. I will say it now and forever but the Dark Tower. The source material is legendary among Stephen King fans, and there's absolutely nothing wrong with the actors. It just was so poorly executed
  - Which is a shame because Matthew McConaughey was BORN to be Randall Flagg.
5. The Stuff. 

The concept of a dangerous substance being commodified and sold just about everywhere as a snack is quite a good idea on paper, but it was executed in pretty much the same way as every other 80’s B horror.

I still like it though
6. Star Trek V: The Final Frontier 

Interesting concepts on personal pain/healing/adapting. Great performance as a radical emotional Vulcan by character Sybok but as a whole the movie broke too many of its own rules. Not so great movie but with charm.
7. The sci fi one about people living on time displayed on their wrists with Justin Timberlake

## 5. 12 years of Breaking Bad: Ozymandias The highest-rated episode in television history ever.

**Score:** 517

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. * You killed Hank…
* No… Nooo.. NOOOOO! I tried to save him!


That line was delivered perfectly.


Also while he was on the phone with Skylar. When he was sounding tough and menacing (in order to absolve her involvement) but crying the whole time.
  - Absolute masterclass
    - The phone call scene might be the finest peace of acting all time
2. This episode was so good it made me stupid. When Walt Jr grabbed the knife, I yelled “he’s gonna kill his mom?!” And then immediately realized that made no sense.
3. SCIENCE BITCH!
4. Best television show ever made. Easily.
  - I would actually say Vince one upped himself with Better Call Saul (which was even better because Breaking Bad was Amazing).


But I think The Wire is probably the best show ever.
    - Yeah I used to think BB was the best show ever then I watched The Wire and The Sopranos. But without BB I wouldn’t have watched those shows.
5. The one where Holly escapes.
6. I’m more of a “Fly” apologist.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Spike Lee's reactionary turn in Highest 2 Lowest

**Score:** 96
**Body:** Spike Lee’s latest film Highest to Lowest demonstrates a reactionary turn in his filmmaking, especially when placed side by side with Kurosawa’s original. Kurosawa’s story revolved around the precarious paradise of the protagonist, whose world of order and ambition is shattered when the hell of a fractured society intrudes violently into his life. The tension came from this constant collision between individual aspiration and collective breakdown. Lee in contrast shifts the focus toward a self-indulgent portrait of the character played by Denzel Washington. He is presented as a Black man who has made it, who embodies the triumph of elevating Black culture to its highest form, besieged not by the structural forces of oppression but by a hostile world reduced mainly to decadent culture.

The enemy in this story is not systemic racism or economic domination but the degeneration of rap music, portrayed as an art form corrupted by its obsession with success and empty fame. Against this backdrop stands the woman Washington’s character notices at the end, who symbolizes purity and a redemptive alternative. This framework produces a deeply conservative image: a dignified, accomplished Black artist surrounded by vulgarized popular culture, longing for an idealized personal salvation.

What is striking is the almost complete absence of class discourse. There is little or no attempt to frame the character’s situation in relation to the contradictions of capitalism, or to link his isolation to the ongoing exploitation of Black communities. Instead, the film indulges in a reactionary critique of rap, of youth culture, and of social media, suggesting that the true danger lies in the supposedly corrosive values of the younger generation.

I had expected the film to take aim at the Black bourgeoisie, to interrogate the illusions of the “liberal” upper-middle-class dream and to remind us that racial domination in the United States continues even after the victories of the civil rights movement. But this expectation is displaced by a melodramatic and depoliticized celebration of artistic passion. Rather than exposing the fractures and failures of the post-civil rights era, Spike Lee offers us a comforting and ultimately conservative narrative of artistic transcendence, one that turns away from the structural realities of inequality and opts instead for an individualized drama of culture and redemption.

And don't get me started about that elevator soundtrack. It ruins nearly every scene.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Interesting take, I think another issue for the film when compared to the original is that the original film wasn’t afraid to sideline Mifune for significant portions of the runtime and that opened up the opportunity to explore certain issues more deeply. The remake hardly takes the camera off Denzel and as a result I found it very one dimensional in comparison.
  - Nakadai and Yamazaki are so, so critical to pulling off the genre hopping feast of High and Low.
2. >What is striking is the almost complete absence of class discourse. There is little or no attempt to frame the character’s situation in relation to the contradictions of capitalism, or to link his isolation to the ongoing exploitation of Black communities.

>Spike Lee offers us a comforting and ultimately conservative narrative of artistic transcendence, one that turns away from the structural realities of inequality and opts instead for an individualized drama of culture and redemption.

This is really astute and gets at why the film feels so...pat. I try not to compare remakes to the original on first viewing but it was really hard not to notice how Kurosawa's (self-reflective) guilt trip about the abandonment of an entire class of people by their now-rich former neighbours post-WWII was completely abandoned. The entire point of the film is just gone here.

Why? I'd love to know. I'm not sue I've ever seen a Lee film so in love with its protagonist. Washington's King is basically flawless and given such a happy ending. Literally the opposite of Kurosawa's which still redeems Kingo but only because it completely humbles him. Kurosawa understood Kingo was not individually a bad guy but he was complicit in the system. That's the entire point of the central plot point with the switched kids! Lee seems unable to criticize King because he thinks "Hey, this guy isn't bad...he deserves to be happy." Never mind the system.
3. I somewhat disagree. The film misses a lot of possible commentary it can make on the failures of black capitalism but I wouldn’t call it fully reactionary. I don’t think the youth stuff is there just to say “youth is bad.” There seems to be the intention of making a commentary on black capitalism (I can see all the pieces being placed there for that purpose) but it just ends up saying absolutely nothing progressive or reactionary. It’s just a pure bumbling mess of a film in this way. 

To call it reactionary implies that it’s saying anything coherent at all which I just don’t think is the case.
4. I just thought it sucked…

Heh, no but seriously interesting take. I was so floored by disappointment with this film for so many reasons that I barely could think about it without being almost angry at how much Spike blew an amazing chance to say something. I was too pissed to actually think about what I was hoping for it to be and this would have been an excellent angle to go from. Instead he just threw all of his over-enthusiasm for the things he loves and we end up with a teenager who doesn’t know who Michael (Jordan) is, but he has a Kamala poster on his wall..
  - This movie was pretty terrible. It made me wistful for not-so-great Spike Lee films like “Son of Sam.” This feels like a guy who, like Denzel in the film, has completely lost touch with normal people. It’s almost autobiographical. And the way they figure out who the kidnapper is (because a song was playing?) was straight stupid.
    - And what the hell was that whole music video for? Like what did that add!? I was so wholeheartedly disappointed in that film and its complete lack of cohesive vision. Didn’t say anyyyyything. It was as dumb as the money thieves who pass the bag from a scooter going North to a scooter going back South where they came from. I’m mad all over again.
5. Yeah... It has some cool scenes, some good performances, but overall it's grating and confusing and worst of all, as you say, a disappointing turn towards conservatism. I don't even blame Spike for struggling to grasp youth culture, what's so mind boggling is that the class conflict is already there in the original and such obvious rich material to be updated for our deeply politically contentious times. And I don't fault him for his stylistic quirks, I like some of his other weirder late career movies that he's made- even if I don't fully grasp the sensibility I can appreciate that he's being true to his own sense of expression. But this one is such a perfect setup for him to make something great, something he's still capable of doing (BlacKkKlansman), and it ends up just revealing how incredibly out of touch he is with the current political climate.
  - I'll give Spike a little benefit of the doubt because he didn't write it, some white guy whose only other credit is an 8 year old comedy show with a typo in the title did. Which makes Spike look like he's bad at picking projects more than he is just completely off his rocker. He's always been a little all over the place artistically, and for every couple Malcolm Xes or BlacKkKlansmans you end up with a She Hate Me or a Da Sweet Blood of Jesus here and there.
6. It’s just a bad movie. It’s a movie of the week for hallmark. It’s laughably bad. The acting is like a bad broadway play. Everything about it is just so bad. I’m mostly just upset that I was tricked into watching it.

## 2. What do you find to be the most tasteless/egregious thing or technique Filmmakers do?

**Score:** 55
**Body:** For me, it would have to be using real CCTV footage or real footage of disasters/atrocities in a film to create a sense of urgency and realism in a fictional scenario. This for me extends to the opening credits sequence in The Purge (2013), the Recent War of the Worlds movie, they used actual war, disaster and other images and CGI'd Aliens into them, even going so far as to recreate footage of an actual plane crash in Afghanistan on the Producer's insistence. 

Something about using this kind of footage, to try and hone in on the severity of the scenario, just never sat right with me. This isn't just really vague footage that's just static or even stock footage, no this is actual footage of people being killed, bombed in a real war, and being victims of shootings in a for-profit film is just enraging.

It's one thing to use news footage and edit a few things here and there, but to use war footage as VFX shots and showing real people getting killed, is the type of the shit that can get you fired and Blacklisted. Or at the very least it should.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I like your CCTV example, so my example would be the reverse. I've noticed that at times when the MCU wants to show a "recorded" version of a prior event, they show actual clips from the former film, and not a different angle from a cctv camera.
  - This! I hate when CCTV, dreams or memories are recycled footage. It does not work.
2. In films based on real life, it's incredibly tasteless when real people are depicted as baddies just to fit them into the narrative of the film. 

The Lost King (2022) paints the real life University of Leicester archaeologists as baddies, including using their actual names. Just so they could turn a fascinating real-life story into yet another tedious narrative of the plucky amateur who follows her heart and is better at archaeology than those stuffy "experts".

It seems so easily avoidable. Why not just use a fictional university and/or create some completely made-up characters, rather than defaming the characters of real people? Some of those real people have commenced legal action against the producers, and quite right too.
  - Yea, if I recall correctly another example of this is in Titanic. In the movie, the first mate panics and ends up shooting someone as people are trying to get into the lifeboats but this caused a huge controversy because in actual accounts he was extremely brave and helped save people and willingly gave up his own seat on a lifeboat.
  - In United 93 they made the real life German man who was on the plane into a coward who tried to stop them rushing the cockpit. 
There is no evidence whatsoever that this happened and was purely made up for dramatic effect. 
Of course they couldn't use an American passenger in this fashion so picked the foreigner. 
Im not sure but I think his family sued the producers. If they didn't they should have.
3. I am a big Haneke fan but have always had trouble with how he kills real animals in almost all his movies. Seventh Continent, Happy End, Cache, Time of the Wolf, probably a few others I'm forgetting.
4. I'm really not big on them using CGI to de-age old actors, like we've been casting young actors to play the younger versions of older characters for decades, and it works fine. Now we get shit like The Irishman where you see a 20yr old with a CGI face hobbling around with the body of a 70 year old man, it's completely ridiculous and pure vanity on the part of an actor to think they can play a character 50 years younger than they actually are.
  - Definitely. It's distracting in every way possible. It looks weird when you're watching the film. And when you're reading ABOUT the film, all they talk about is the technology used. 

Just cast younger/older actors to play a younger/older version of the character! We all understand and accept this, and no-one thinks it's weird. There's also the added bonus that the film won't look like crap in a few years because the effects have aged badly.
    - I mean the effects looked weird and like crap on release, I’m guessing in a few years they will be unwatchable 
5. First off, this is a really thoughtful question and post and I'm really liking reading the discussion here. Just to add another one, how therapy is portrayed in most movies tends to be pretty frustrating. It's so common to show a therapist doing something completely inappropriate and acting like it's good therapy and/or a normal thing to do

## 3. Michael Mann, Blackhat [Director's Cut]. Perfection

**Score:** 36
**Body:** TLDR: DC Blackhat, a superb edit. 
There's this long scene in Micheal Mann's Director's Cut of *Black Hat* featuring Viola Davis and Christopher Hemsworth. The set-piece where everyone gets to shine as an actor. It opens with Chris Hemsworth coming out of these curtains in a restaurant,  a grin upon their face, a fox easily returning from an unguarded chicken-coop. In a busy restaurant. They and the gang hope they're on the right trail. 

There's some noticeable camp to it. Cartoonny prison negotiations. Anime dating-drama. A car trip to "nowhere?" There is schmaltz. There are silly moments in the CQC with table legs. The culminating "local" disaster is infected with reminders of artificiality of experience. *Allan please add details* 

Theatrical Cut being griped for poor story sequencing; begins at 11 and can't carry it to the crescendo. Directos Cut sequences the roller coaster correctly; Begins at a 6 and then goes nuclear before going off the scales in a build up that comes more smoothly.

And then there are moments of pure magic. The composition of a boat at sea. The endearing electrical signals traveling through systems. The monochrome heist in white; A variation of a theme. An accompaniment to the heist scenes in *Heat.* A supernova on a sidewalk

In this one scene Mann creates a crescendo of energy, Atoms bouncing, colliding. But for a moment you can see the center of the universe at creation where time is nothing, all slows. 

Viola Davis [one of the greats], not one of the good ones (but trying) getting the last say. The eyes. Such catharsis. The skyscrapers. Her Peace.

Holt McCallany, one of the good ones(cops), competent. Absolutely owns his moments with the assuredness. Against all odds, he evened em' with nothing but a squirt gun. Absolutely does Justice to embodying a character built by the hands of the deities just for that moment in this wonderful scene. His Peace. Absolutely Blown away. 

DC rendition also slightly alters the aftermath of the second act of this scene. In the TC, the score lacks cohesiveness with the beat of the emotion of the scene. *It's a beat in the wrong West German Diskothek.*

For the DC, the music is subtly altered to a new piece. Matches the quiet frantic flight for survival. The long well lit tunnels, open barrels. The clear windows, shooting galleries. Richie Coster an undeterred zealot, fast behind them. Chris Hemsworth and Tang Wei, make real this chemistry befitting of a Mann drama.

Only when they reach the portal does Mann allow the scene and audience to breathe in small relief. But there is still fear; Deer escaping wildfire. 

Initially scoffed at for outlandish plots, made only frightening in years of recent. 

Not usually rated highly in MM's filmography; a mistake.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Black Hat renaissance is upon us! I love this movie. The dialogue straddles the line of being over dramatic yet grounded. I believe these characters. I think the criticism of casting Chris Hemsworth as a hacker is unfounded. He’s a lot of fun to watch. 

Special shout out to the sound design in this movie. The scene where they are having a gunfight in the tunnel, the gunshots sound intense.
2. Thanks! from the outset something was so off that I stopped watching the theatrical release cut after ten minutes. Will look it up. A reminder that senseless editing can kill any level of talent

## 4. The best way to watch Dekalog

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I am interested in watching Dekalog and I am curious to know the best way to watch it. I've seen Dekalog referred to as both a 10 hour film and a mini series comprising 10 one hour long films. Therefore, is it better to set some time aside over a couple of days and watch it as one long film or is it better to watch one or two episodes per day? Did anyone start out with the intention of watching it like a series, but enjoyed it so much that they ended up binging the whole thing inn a short space of time? From what I gather, you can also watch each episode in any order, so I am curious to see if there is a consensus on the best approach for a first viewing



### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. They're individual episodes. I'd recommend watching it spaced out, but that's just me. You can watch it at the pace that makes most sense to you. Start with the first episode and see if you want to continue right after, or if you want to soak it in first.


You also have the possibility to watch the episodes " A short film about love" "and "A short film about killing" in longer versions around 90minutes.
  - Thanks for your response. I understand that Dekalog has quite a bit of symbolism, so I am wondering if it makes sense to watch like a series and allowing myself time to process/ savour the experience. I have heard about the 90 episodes. Are they worth watching for first viewing or saving them for a rewatch?
    - I personally don't think the extended cinematic versions add anything essential (though they are good films). Don't sit around worrying about "symbolism", just enjoy it on the level, it's an emotional/moral/spiritual show more than anything.
2. We watched it over a couple of weeks, one episode at a time. It’s a mini series but the subject matter and symbolism is so deep and detailed that you should just treat them as 10 short films.

I think it’s better to spread them out and have some time to digest each one. The first one is probably going to leave you wanting time to digest anyway!
  - Thanks. I figured this may be the case. I am a huge fan of long films like Out 1 and La Flor,  but there is something about Dekalog that is giving me the sense that I should spread the whole thing out
3. Just start watching and if you feel like watching another, do. It's a thematic whole comprised of ten individiual films, with different cinematographers. It certainly makes sense to watch them all, but I can't imagine there's any benefit in approaching it as a marathon - better to give each one some time to settle in, especially first time round. I'd stick to the established order on first viewing, because why mess with it? Set aside all concerns, watch the first and then take it from there. When you've watched them all, then think about how you might want to revisit them (the longer versions of Killing and Love might be your first port of call).
4. For me at least there was a benefit to splitting it up. It was a pragmatic choice but with the benefit that some time between segments allowed me to mull over. Some were far more memorable than others. I still think about this film, nice to hear about others interested in it.
5. I think you can watch them in any order - there are some recurring characters and background 'presences' but I didn't detect any particular narrative to these such that watching them in any order would matter. But I would agree with the other poster: give each of them a little time to sink in. They're thematically and emotionally rich. It's the best TV series I've ever seen, but I would never binge-watch it. I'm not even sure I could!

## 5. The Godfather — Got To Watch It On The Big Screen

**Score:** 7
**Body:** What’s to say that hasn’t been said already about this film. This was my second watch and what an experience!

On this watch I was so caught up by the performances. The way Brando conveys power through every gesture and in the cadence of his speech. Vito is never in a hurry. He knows everyone else is waiting on him. He can take as much time as he wants. And maybe that’s where he faltered.

The way Coppola and Al Pacino develop Michael’s character is incredible. The scene where Michael works up the courage to actually pull the trigger in the restaurant, conveyed solely through Al Pacino’s eyes and sound design is, again, so good. 

It’s not just in the big moments. Every single one of these actors live and breathe their characters all the time. 

I noticed or remembered other details this time.

How Michael partly resembles Vito with half his face swollen in the scene where he first makes a decision on behalf of the family to kill Sollozzo and McCluskey.

How the film has a way of making each location feel tactile, like you could reach through the screen and feel the texture. On this watch I noticed, in the scene where they’re waiting for confirmation of the restaurant, Coppola puts everyone in a single frame under the light and doesn’t move the camera. The phone rings. Sunny gets up and walks away from the camera towards the phone. He doesn’t cut to a close up of Sunny on the phone. We hear Sunny’s conversation on the phone from a distance. Then he walks all the way back into the light.

It really makes the whole space feel real. This film is full of choices like this. It’s like the filmmaking doesn’t get in its own way. 

I kept thinking how a modern version of this scene would’ve chopped everything up. All the characters would be siloed into their own close ups. I’m not saying film language shouldn’t evolve. There’s definitely a place for faster pacing and non ensemble staging. When long takes are forced it can get a little annoying too. It’s just that when a filmmaker knows exactly where to put the camera for a whole scene it hits different.
